cards.question: State the three types of intermolecular force in order of increasing strength.
cards.answer: Induced dipole–dipole (London) forces < permanent dipole–dipole < hydrogen bonding.
markPoints: London (induced dipole) < permanent dipole–dipole < hydrogen bonding
cards.explanation: All are weak compared with covalent/ionic bonds, but hydrogen bonds are the strongest IMF.

cards.question: State the condition for hydrogen bonding to occur.
cards.answer: Hydrogen must be bonded directly to a highly electronegative atom with a lone pair: nitrogen, oxygen or fluorine (N, O or F).
markPoints: H bonded to N, O or F (with a lone pair)
cards.explanation: Only N, O, F are electronegative enough. The H bonds to a lone pair on N/O/F of a neighbouring molecule.

cards.question: Explain how London (induced dipole–dipole) forces arise.
cards.answer: The random movement of electrons creates an instantaneous (temporary) dipole in a molecule. This induces a dipole in a neighbouring molecule. The δ+ and δ− of the temporary and induced dipoles attract each other.
markPoints: electron movement gives an instantaneous/temporary dipole
this induces a dipole in a neighbouring molecule
attraction between the temporary and induced dipoles
cards.explanation: Three steps: instantaneous dipole → induced dipole → attraction. They get stronger with more electrons.

cards.question: Explain why the boiling points of the noble gases increase down Group 0 (He → Xe).
cards.answer: Down the group the atoms have more electrons, so the London (induced dipole) forces between atoms are stronger. More energy is needed to overcome them, so the boiling point increases.
markPoints: more energy needed to overcome them
cards.explanation: More electrons = larger instantaneous dipoles = stronger IMFs = higher boiling point.

cards.question: Explain why ammonia (NH₃) has a higher boiling point than phosphine (PH₃), even though PH₃ has more electrons.
cards.answer: NH₃ can form hydrogen bonds (N–H bonded to N) whereas PH₃ cannot (P is not electronegative enough). Hydrogen bonds are stronger than the permanent dipole–dipole/London forces in PH₃, so more energy is needed to separate NH₃ molecules.
markPoints: NH₃ has hydrogen bonding; PH₃ does not
hydrogen bonds are stronger than the IMFs in PH₃
so NH₃ needs more energy to boil
cards.explanation: Hydrogen bonding overrides the 'more electrons' trend — the classic anomaly to explain.
